heroOverview: Amputation rehabilitation is the specialized medical and therapeutic process of restoring function, mobility, and independence to individuals following surgical limb amputation. Approximately 185,000 amputations are performed in the United States each year, with lower-limb amputations accounting for 65-70%, predominantly driven by peripheral artery disease and diabetes. Rehabilitation begins preoperatively whenever possible and continues through residual limb healing, prosthetic fitting, gait training, and community reintegration. At one year, 60-70% of below-knee amputees achieve functional prosthetic ambulation; above-knee amputees achieve functional ambulation in 50-60%. Multidisciplinary amputee teams — physiatrist, prosthetist, physical therapist, occupational therapist, psychologist, and peer visitor — produce significantly better outcomes than fragmented single-specialty care.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 185,000 amputations per year in the United States; 2.1 million Americans currently living with limb loss (Ziegler-Graham et al., 2008)
keyStats.demographics: Peripheral artery disease and diabetes account for 54% of lower-limb amputations; trauma accounts for 45% of upper-limb amputations; men represent 70-75% of all amputations
keyStats.avgAge: Dysvascular amputees: median age 65-70; traumatic amputees: median age 30-45; pediatric amputations (congenital and acquired): 2-3 per 10,000 births
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 57.7 million people globally living with limb loss (GBD 2017); landmine-related amputations concentrate in Sub-Saharan Africa and South/Southeast Asia

definition: Amputation rehabilitation (ICD-10: Z89 — acquired absence of limb) is the medical specialty practice of maximizing function, independence, and quality of life following the surgical removal of all or part of a limb. It encompasses the full care continuum from pre-amputation counseling and surgical planning through post-operative residual limb management, prosthetic prescription and fitting, functional training, and long-term community integration. Amputations are classified by anatomical level: for the lower extremity, partial foot, transtibial (below-knee or BK), transfemoral (above-knee or AK), hip disarticulation, and hemipelvectomy; for the upper extremity, partial hand, transradial (below-elbow), transhumeral (above-elbow), shoulder disarticulation, and forequarter. Level of amputation is the single most important determinant of rehabilitation outcome: transtibial amputees require approximately twice the energy expenditure of normal gait; transfemoral amputees require 65% more energy. Physical medicine and rehabilitation (PM&R) physicians (physiatrists) lead the interdisciplinary rehabilitation team, collaborating with prosthetists for device prescription, physical therapists for mobility training, occupational therapists for upper-limb function and ADL training, psychologists for adjustment and phantom limb pain, and vocational rehabilitation specialists for return to work.

typesClassification.description: The most functionally favorable lower-limb amputation level. The retained knee joint dramatically reduces energy expenditure of prosthetic ambulation compared with above-knee levels. 65-70% achieve K3-K4 functional ambulation. Rehabilitation focuses on residual limb shaping, socket fit, and gait pattern optimization.

Loss of the knee joint significantly increases energy expenditure. Microprocessor-controlled knees (MPK) reduce fall risk and improve community ambulation in eligible patients. A higher proportion of dysvascular and elderly patients do not achieve outdoor functional prosthetic ambulation, and wheelchair-dependent mobility is planned for alongside prosthetics.

Retention of the elbow significantly improves prosthetic function. Body-powered hooks and myoelectric hands are the two primary prosthetic modalities. Rehabilitation focuses on control training, functional grip strategies, and bimanual activities. High user satisfaction and return-to-work rates compared with higher levels.

Loss of the elbow joint substantially complicates prosthetic control. Hybrid prostheses combining body power at the elbow with myoelectric terminal devices are the most functional option. Targeted muscle reinnervation (TMR) and osseointegration are emerging surgical techniques that dramatically improve prosthetic control and sensory feedback.

typesClassification.type: Dysvascular amputation (peripheral artery disease or diabetic)
typesClassification.description: The majority of lower-limb amputations in high-income countries. Rehabilitation is more complex due to comorbidities (heart disease, renal failure, neuropathy, poor skin integrity). Prosthetic candidacy is assessed against cardiovascular fitness, contralateral limb status, and cognitive function. Contralateral limb preservation is a rehabilitation priority.

primarySymptoms: Residual limb pain from surgical wound, suture tension, neuroma formation, or skin breakdown at prosthetic socket contact points — the most common early rehabilitation challenge.
Phantom limb sensation — the perception that the missing limb is still present, experienced by 80-90% of amputees and ranging from non-painful awareness to vivid movement sensation.

Phantom limb pain — painful sensation perceived in the absent limb, affecting 60-80% of amputees, ranging from intermittent cramping or burning to continuous severe pain that significantly impairs rehabilitation engagement.

Residual limb volume fluctuation — edema, limb shrinkage, and day-to-day volume changes that affect prosthetic socket fit, cause skin breakdown, and require frequent prosthetic adjustments particularly in the first year.
Skin breakdown, pressure ulcers, and blistering at the prosthetic socket interface — the most common reason for prosthetic non-use in both lower and upper limb amputees.
Impaired balance and fall risk during prosthetic gait training and daily activities, particularly in elderly and above-knee amputees.
Reduced aerobic capacity and early fatigue from increased energy cost of prosthetic ambulation and deconditioning from prolonged bed rest.
Upper limb amputation: loss of grip, pinch, and fine motor function affecting writing, eating, dressing, and occupational tasks, with significant psychosocial impact.
Psychological adjustment difficulties — depression, anxiety, body image disturbance, and grief over limb loss — present in 25-50% of amputees and requiring specialist psychological support.
Contralateral limb overloading — knee, hip, and foot pain in the remaining limb from compensatory loading during prosthetic gait, a significant long-term complication.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive residual limb swelling and skin discoloration suggesting wound dehiscence, hematoma, or deep infection requiring surgical review.
Increasing wound or residual limb pain beyond expected post-operative trajectory — may indicate wound complication, osteomyelitis, or neuroma formation.
New phantom limb pain emerging or intensifying after initial improvement — may reflect residual limb complications, poor socket fit, or psychological triggers requiring re-assessment.

Contralateral limb pain or skin breakdown in a dysvascular or diabetic amputee — the contralateral limb is at extremely high risk and loss within 3-5 years occurs in 25-50% without aggressive preventive care.
Psychological deterioration — withdrawal, refusal to engage in rehabilitation, and signs of clinical depression — should prompt immediate psychological assessment and intervention.
emergencySigns: Signs of residual limb infection: spreading erythema, purulent discharge, fever above 38.5°C, and increasing pain beyond 1-2 weeks post-operatively — requires immediate surgical review to exclude necrotizing fasciitis.
Sudden loss of vascular supply to the residual limb — pale, pulseless, or cold stump — indicates arterial compromise requiring emergency vascular surgery.
Cauda equina syndrome or progressive neurological deficit in bilateral or proximal amputees from prolonged immobility or spinal complications.
Severe prosthetic-related skin breakdown with deep ulceration — halts rehabilitation and requires immediate prosthetist and wound care specialist review to prevent sepsis.

causes.cause: Peripheral artery disease (PAD) and diabetes mellitus
causes.description: The leading cause of lower-limb amputation in high-income countries, accounting for approximately 54% of US cases. PAD causes critical limb ischemia with tissue necrosis or gangrene when revascularization is no longer feasible. Diabetic neuropathy, infection, and Charcot arthropathy compound ischemia, leading to progressive foot and leg tissue loss requiring amputation.

Severe trauma from motor vehicle accidents, industrial machinery, blast injuries (military and improvised explosive devices), and falls causes limb loss when the degree of vascular, bone, and soft tissue injury precludes limb salvage. Traumatic amputees are typically younger and have better rehabilitation outcomes than dysvascular amputees.

causes.cause: Cancer requiring limb-sparing failure or salvage procedure
causes.description: Primary bone and soft tissue sarcomas (osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, synovial sarcoma) occasionally require amputation when limb-salvage surgery has failed or when the tumor location or size precludes safe margin-negative resection. Forequarter and hindquarter amputations are predominantly oncological indications.

Necrotizing fasciitis, osteomyelitis, and gas gangrene from severe infections may require emergency amputation to control life-threatening sepsis. Diabetic foot infections progressing to deep-space abscess and bone infection are the most common infectious amputation indication.

Congenital limb absence or severe hypoplasia at birth affects 2-3 per 10,000 live births. Some children undergo planned amputation or amputation equivalent surgery (such as Syme amputation) to optimize prosthetic fitting. Pediatric amputee rehabilitation differs fundamentally from adult rehabilitation, with growth considerations guiding prosthetic prescription.

riskFactors.factor: Diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and foot ulceration
riskFactors.description: Poorly controlled diabetes is the leading single risk factor for lower-limb amputation; the lifetime risk of amputation in diabetic patients is 25-30× higher than in non-diabetics. Annual diabetic foot exams and aggressive wound management reduce amputation rates by 45-85%.

Ankle-brachial index below 0.4 with critical limb ischemia doubles the one-year amputation risk; revascularization when feasible dramatically reduces amputation need. Risk increases with smoking, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and diabetes.

Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for PAD and significantly impairs wound healing after amputation, increasing stump complications, prosthetic fitting failure rates, and contralateral amputation risk. Cessation is the single most impactful preventive intervention.

Blast injuries from improvised explosive devices produce complex polytrauma amputations with multiple-limb involvement. The Walter Reed Army Medical Center amputee program documents over 1,700 service members receiving amputations from combat injuries since 2001.

Age above 65 is associated with lower prosthetic candidacy, reduced rehabilitation tolerance, and higher contralateral limb loss rates. Medicare K-level assignment shows that 30-40% of elderly dysvascular amputees achieve only limited (K1-K2) or no prosthetic ambulation.

Cognitive impairment reduces capacity to learn new gait patterns and increases fall risk; depression reduces rehabilitation engagement, physical activity, and outcomes. Both should be screened and treated as part of amputation rehabilitation planning.

Bilateral lower-limb amputees require prosthetics at both levels; energy expenditure rises steeply. In bilateral transfemoral amputees, wheelchair-dependent mobility is realistic for most older adults alongside prosthetic mobility goals.

affectedDemographics: Men aged 30-60 (traumatic amputation): men represent 70-75% of all amputations; blast injury amputation disproportionately affects military-age men.
Adults aged 65 and above with diabetes and PAD: the fastest-growing amputee demographic; comorbidities substantially affect rehabilitation tolerance and prosthetic outcomes.

Sub-Saharan Africa and South/Southeast Asia: landmine injuries and complications of inadequately managed diabetes account for disproportionately high amputation rates with limited rehabilitation access.

Children with congenital limb deficiency: unique rehabilitation challenges related to growth, developmental milestones, and age-appropriate prosthetic goals; managed by specialized pediatric amputee centers.
diagnosisOverview: Amputation rehabilitation begins with a comprehensive functional assessment by a physiatrist and the interdisciplinary team. The assessment covers: amputation level and residual limb status (length, shape, skin integrity, presence of edema or pain); cardiovascular fitness (6-minute walk test, VO2 max estimate); cognitive function (MoCA or MMSE for elderly patients); psychological state (PHQ-9 for depression); prior functional level and living situation; and goals of care. Prosthetic candidacy is determined using the Medicare Functional Classification System (K-levels 0-4), where K2 indicates ability to ambulate on level surfaces at household distances, K3 indicates ability to ambulate outdoors and traverse uneven terrain, and K4 (typical for traumatic amputees) indicates high-activity prosthetic use. Residual limb assessment includes circumferential measurement for volume tracking, skin integrity inspection for breakdown or infection, stump length and bony prominence identification. Vascular status of the residual limb and contralateral limb is assessed by pulse examination, ABI, and Doppler in dysvascular patients. Wound healing is monitored clinically and by surgical team review; premature prosthetic fitting before complete wound healing risks dehiscence.

diagnosticTests.test: Residual limb assessment and circumferential measurement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Tracks residual limb maturation and volume stability for prosthetic fitting timing; identifies skin breakdown and bony prominences that affect socket design
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The prosthetist or physiotherapist takes circumferential measurements at standardized intervals along the residual limb using a tape measure. Takes 10-15 minutes. Performed at each clinic visit in the first 6 months.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardized assessment of prosthetic candidacy and rehabilitation potential; determines the appropriate prosthetic components prescribed and insurance coverage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The rehabilitation physician evaluates gait history, comorbidities, cognitive status, motivation, and functional goals. Results in a K0-K4 classification that determines prosthetic prescription and funding eligibility.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses cardiovascular fitness and prosthetic ambulation endurance; outcome measure for tracking rehabilitation progress
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient walks as far as possible in 6 minutes on a flat surface. Distance in meters is recorded and compared with reference values for amputee populations. Takes 6 minutes plus setup time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses peripheral vascular disease severity in the remaining limb to guide preventive care and predict contralateral amputation risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood pressure cuffs applied to the arm and ankle; Doppler probe used to detect pulse signals. Takes 10-15 minutes. ABI under 0.9 indicates PAD requiring aggressive management.

diagnosticTests.test: Psychological assessment (PHQ-9, Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies depression and anxiety that impair rehabilitation engagement, are prevalent in 25-50% of new amputees, and require concurrent treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Self-administered questionnaires, 5-10 minutes. Positive screens are followed by psychological assessment and referral.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for cognitive impairment that affects ability to learn prosthetic use and follow safety protocols, particularly relevant in elderly dysvascular amputees
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-item cognitive test administered by a clinician over 10 minutes. Score under 26 suggests possible cognitive impairment requiring further evaluation.

treatmentOverview: Amputation rehabilitation follows a staged progression from acute post-operative care through advanced prosthetic training. Pre-operative rehabilitation education — covering expected recovery milestones, prosthetic options, and peer visitation from experienced amputees — significantly reduces post-operative anxiety and improves engagement. Post-operative care priorities are wound healing, edema control with rigid removable dressings or shrinker socks, pain management (residual limb and phantom limb), and early mobilization on crutches or a wheelchair. Phantom limb pain management begins immediately: mirror visual feedback therapy, transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, and medications (gabapentin 300-1,200 mg three times daily as first-line, amitriptyline 10-75 mg nightly as second-line) reduce severity. Preprosthetic physiotherapy strengthens the residual limb and intact extremities, improves cardiovascular fitness, and trains wheelchair skills and transfers. Prosthetic fitting begins when the residual limb is well-healed, volume-stable, and shaped — typically 4-8 weeks post-operatively. Initial prostheses use preparatory components; definitive, activity-appropriate prostheses are fitted after gait pattern is established. Gait training progresses from parallel bars to indoor ambulation, outdoor surfaces, stairs, and community environments. K3-K4 patients are trained in running, sports, and high-demand activities using activity-specific prostheses. Upper limb amputees receive occupational therapy focusing on bimanual function training, prosthetic component control, and activities of daily living. The ISPO and AOPA provide guidelines for prosthetic prescription standards.

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin (300-1,200 mg three times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacotherapy for phantom limb pain and residual limb neuropathic pain. Reduces ectopic discharge from injured afferents and dorsal horn sensitization. Titrate gradually to minimize dizziness and sedation. Also reduces acute post-operative opioid requirements when started preoperatively.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces phantom limb pain intensity by 30-50% vs placebo in RCTs; NNT of 5-7 for clinically meaningful pain relief

medicalTreatments.name: Mirror visual feedback therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Patient observes the intact limb's reflection, creating visual illusion of the missing limb moving without pain. Repeated sessions of 15-20 minutes reduce phantom limb pain through visual-motor cortical reorganization mechanisms. Self-administered at home after initial training.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant reduction in phantom limb pain in 50-65% of patients in systematic reviews; Cochrane evidence supports over sham therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to the residual limb to reduce phantom and residual limb neuropathic pain via gate-control mechanisms. Non-invasive, self-administered, and compatible with other phantom pain interventions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest but consistent pain reduction as adjunct therapy; preferred as low-risk first-line physical modality for pain

medicalTreatments.name: Tricyclic antidepressants — amitriptyline (10-75 mg nightly)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line pharmacotherapy for phantom limb pain with concurrent sleep disturbance and depression. Nortriptyline preferred in older adults for lower anticholinergic burden. Onset of analgesia 2-4 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT of 3-4 for neuropathic pain reduction; additionally treats depression comorbid in 25-50% of amputees

medicalTreatments.name: Opioids (short-term perioperative or breakthrough)
medicalTreatments.description: Appropriate for acute post-operative pain management and short-term breakthrough phantom pain; not recommended as long-term treatment for phantom limb pain given limited evidence and addiction risk. Opioid weaning should be planned from the earliest rehabilitation stage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for acute pain; evidence for long-term phantom pain relief modest and inferior to gabapentinoids

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin injection or targeted muscle reinnervation (TMR)
medicalTreatments.description: Botulinum toxin injection into painful neuromas provides 3-6 months of pain relief for refractory residual limb neuroma pain. TMR is a surgical technique rerouting residual nerves to available muscle targets, reducing neuroma formation and enabling improved myoelectric prosthetic control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Neuroma pain reduction in 60-70% of botulinum toxin-treated patients; TMR reduces neuroma pain in 80-90% and significantly improves myoelectric prosthetic control

surgicalOptions.name: Targeted muscle reinnervation (TMR)
surgicalOptions.description: Reinnervates residual nerve branches to nearby muscle targets, preventing neuroma formation and creating myoelectric signals that improve intuitive prosthetic control. Increasingly performed at the time of amputation rather than as a secondary procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces phantom pain and neuroma pain in 80-90% of patients; improves myoelectric prosthetic control speed and number of functional grips in upper-limb amputees in multiple cohort studies

surgicalOptions.name: Osseointegration (bone-anchored prosthetic implant)
surgicalOptions.description: A titanium implant is inserted into the medullary canal of the residual bone and protrudes through the skin as a percutaneous anchor for direct prosthetic attachment, eliminating the socket interface entirely. Substantially reduces skin breakdown and socket discomfort; improves proprioception via osseoperception.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 70-85% return to full-time prosthetic use with high satisfaction; implant survival rate 90% at 5 years in major series; infection rate 10-20% requiring antibiotic management but rarely implant removal

surgicalOptions.name: Residual limb revision surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Bone-end trimming, myoplasty, or skin revision for non-healing wounds, problematic bony prominences, or redundant tissue that prevents comfortable socket fit. Typically performed 3-6 months post-amputation once the limb has matured.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% improvement in socket tolerance and prosthetic fit after revision; lower re-revision rates with physiatrist and prosthetist input guiding surgical planning

alternativeTreatments.name: Virtual reality-based mirror therapy and phantom limb pain treatment
alternativeTreatments.description: Immersive VR environments deliver enhanced mirror feedback and graded motor imagery for phantom limb pain. Small RCTs show superior pain reduction compared with traditional mirror therapy, with particular benefit for complex upper-limb phantoms.

alternativeTreatments.name: Graded motor imagery (GMI) program
alternativeTreatments.description: Three-phase protocol: limb laterality recognition, imagined movements, and mirror therapy. Validated in complex regional pain syndrome and increasingly used for phantom limb pain, with systematic review evidence supporting pain reduction without adverse effects.

whySeeSpecialist: A physiatrist (PM&R physician) specializing in amputee rehabilitation should be involved from the pre-operative phase for elective amputations and within days of emergency amputations. Physiatrists coordinate prosthetic prescription, pain management, and the interdisciplinary rehabilitation team. Prosthetist involvement is required for all device decisions.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified PM&R physician with documented amputee rehabilitation experience and privileges at an accredited amputee program (CARF-accredited rehabilitation center preferred). Confirm the program employs a certified prosthetist-orthotist (CPO) and has access to microprocessor-controlled prosthetic components and a peer visitor program.
hospitalCriteria: Dedicated amputee rehabilitation team with physiatrist, certified prosthetist, physical therapist, and occupational therapist
CARF-accredited rehabilitation program or equivalent quality-assured rehabilitation unit
Access to a full range of prosthetic componentry including microprocessor-controlled knees and ankles
Psychological services and peer visitor program
Vascular surgery and wound care services for dysvascular amputees
keyFacilities: CARF-accredited amputee rehabilitation programs at major rehabilitation hospitals
Veterans Administration polytrauma rehabilitation centers for combat amputees
Amputee coalition member centers with peer visitor programs
Academic medical center prosthetic research programs for advanced device prescription
preventionStrategies: Achieve optimal blood glucose control (HbA1c under 7%) and annual diabetic foot examination to prevent the diabetic foot ulceration that leads to the majority of dysvascular amputations.
Treat peripheral artery disease aggressively with revascularization when feasible — angioplasty or bypass surgery can rescue limbs in critical ischemia and reduce amputation rates by 50-60%.
Cease smoking to prevent PAD progression and reduce wound healing complications after any vascular or foot procedure.
Perform daily foot inspection and use appropriate protective footwear in all patients with diabetic neuropathy or PAD to detect and treat skin lesions before they progress to gangrene.
Implement landmine clearance and safe road design policies at the community and national level to prevent the trauma-related amputations that disproportionately affect low-income countries.
lifestyleModifications: Follow the prescribed home exercise program daily — residual limb strengthening and cardiovascular conditioning are essential for maintaining prosthetic gait capacity.
Inspect the residual limb and intact limb skin every day, particularly after prosthetic use; report any redness lasting more than 20 minutes, blistering, or breakdown to the prosthetist immediately.
Maintain the prescribed weight using residual limb shrinker socks or an elasticated sheath to manage volume and preserve socket fit, especially in the first year.
Attend all prosthetist appointments for socket adjustments as residual limb volume changes; ill-fitting sockets cause skin breakdown that sets back rehabilitation significantly.

Engage in adaptive sports and social activities — swimming, hand-cycling, wheelchair sports, and amputee-specific running programs — to maintain cardiovascular health, social connection, and body confidence.
Protect the contralateral limb vigilantly — wear appropriate footwear, see a podiatrist annually, and report any foot pain or skin changes immediately in dysvascular or diabetic amputees.

recommended: Mediterranean or plant-forward diet to manage cardiovascular risk factors (PAD, hypertension, hyperlipidemia) underlying the dysvascular amputations
Adequate protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) to support wound healing, residual limb tissue maintenance, and muscle strengthening during rehabilitation
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation where deficient — bone health is important for osseointegration candidates and for avoiding stress fractures in the prosthetic gait
Low-glycaemic-index carbohydrate diet for diabetic amputees to maintain stable blood glucose and support wound healing
avoid: Tobacco in any form — accelerates PAD and dramatically impairs wound healing and contralateral limb vascular status
Excessive alcohol, which increases fall risk in prosthetic users and impairs neuropathic pain management
High-sodium foods that exacerbate hypertension and peripheral edema, worsening residual limb volume control
Crash diets or rapid weight loss, which cause abrupt residual limb volume changes that destabilize socket fit

exerciseGuidelines: Structured cardiovascular training is essential for the increased energy demands of prosthetic ambulation. Transtibial amputees should target 30 minutes of continuous walking-based activity 5 times weekly once gait is established. Transfemoral amputees should engage in pool-based aerobic exercise and seated cardiovascular training while prosthetic gait capacity is developing. Upper-limb amputees should continue aerobic exercise alongside prosthetic training. All amputees benefit from resistance training of residual limb muscles, contralateral limb strengthening, and core stabilization under physiotherapist supervision.
dailyManagement: Wear the prosthesis for increasing daily wear time following the therapist's schedule — building skin tolerance gradually prevents breakdown; record wear time and any discomfort areas.
Perform daily skin inspection of the residual limb using a mirror for hard-to-see areas; remove the prosthesis immediately if skin remains red more than 20 minutes after removal.
Clean the prosthetic liner or suspension system daily with mild soap to prevent microbial growth and skin irritation.
Complete prescribed home exercise program including strength, flexibility, and balance exercises without exception.
Monitor blood glucose (for diabetic amputees) and inspect the contralateral foot daily — the contralateral limb is the single most important limb to protect.
Connect with the peer visitor program or amputee support group monthly — peer support significantly improves psychological adjustment, prosthetic use, and rehabilitation outcomes.
prognosis: Rehabilitation outcomes depend primarily on amputation level, cause (traumatic versus dysvascular), age, comorbidities, and access to quality rehabilitation services. At one year, 60-70% of unilateral transtibial amputees achieve functional prosthetic outdoor ambulation (K3 or above), with return-to-work rates of 40-60% in traumatic amputees under 60. Transfemoral amputees achieve K3 ambulation in 40-55%; microprocessor-controlled knee users have significantly lower fall rates and higher community participation. Dysvascular amputees have a 5-year survival rate of only 40-60%, primarily driven by cardiovascular comorbidity. Contralateral amputation occurs in 25-50% of dysvascular amputees within 3-5 years. Phantom limb pain resolves completely in approximately 30% of patients within one year but persists at moderate severity in 30-50% long-term, requiring ongoing neuropathic pain management. Traumatic amputees, particularly military combat amputees receiving advanced prosthetics and rehabilitation, achieve exceptional functional outcomes — return to running, recreation, and military service in 30-40% of cases at dedicated centers.
recoveryTimeline: Acute wound healing: 4-8 weeks. Residual limb maturation (volume stabilization): 3-6 months. Definitive prosthetic fitting: 4-8 weeks post-amputation for traumatic; 8-16 weeks for dysvascular. Basic prosthetic ambulation: 2-4 months of training. Community ambulation: 4-8 months. Return to work in sedentary roles: 3-6 months; physical roles: 6-18 months.
complications: Phantom limb pain affecting 60-80% of amputees and persisting for years in 30-50% without treatment — managed with gabapentinoids, mirror therapy, and psychological support.

Residual limb neuroma formation — painful nerve-end tumors at the surgical site causing local tenderness and radiating pain — treated with targeted botulinum toxin injection or targeted muscle reinnervation.
Contralateral limb amputation in 25-50% of dysvascular amputees within 3-5 years — prevented by aggressive preventive foot care, vascular surveillance, and diabetic control.
Skin breakdown at the prosthetic socket interface — the most common cause of prosthetic abandonment; prevented by regular socket fitting, skin inspection, and liner hygiene.
Depression and PTSD, particularly in traumatic amputees — present in 25-50% within the first year; require psychologist or psychiatrist co-management alongside physical rehabilitation.

Cardiovascular events (myocardial infarction, stroke) as leading cause of mortality in dysvascular amputees — require aggressive medical management of cardiovascular risk factors throughout rehabilitation.

supportResources.name: Amputee Coalition
supportResources.description: US national patient advocacy organization providing peer visitor programs, support groups, and resources for amputees and their families.

supportResources.name: Orthotics and Prosthetics Alliance (O&P EDGE)
supportResources.description: Prosthetist professional association with a provider locator and patient education materials on prosthetic options.

supportResources.name: Challenged Athletes Foundation
supportResources.description: Provides grants for adaptive sports equipment and programs for amputees and other mobility-impaired athletes.

supportResources.name: DVBIC Amputee Resources (for military veterans)
supportResources.description: US Department of Defense resources for combat amputee rehabilitation, prosthetics, and psychological support.

confusedWithConditions.name: Phantom Limb Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Phantom limb pain is a specific complication of amputation — pain perceived in the absent limb — whereas amputation rehabilitation is the comprehensive process of functional recovery. PHN is a primary diagnosis and treatment target within amputation rehabilitation; its management is addressed as part of the overall rehabilitation plan.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spinal Cord Injury Rehabilitation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Spinal cord injury rehabilitation addresses mobility impairment from neural injury, not limb loss. While both require adaptive equipment and interdisciplinary teams, SCI rehabilitation focuses on neurological recovery, bladder and bowel management, and spasticity — domains not present in isolated amputation rehabilitation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polyneuropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polyneuropathy causes sensory and motor dysfunction in intact limbs; it is frequently a cause of the diabetic foot ulceration that leads to amputation and persists after amputation in the residual limb. In amputation rehabilitation, neuropathy affects prosthetic sensation and contralateral limb risk, but the primary rehabilitation goal is prosthetic function after limb loss.

relatedConditions.name: Phantom Limb Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common pain complication of amputation, present in 60-80% of amputees; management is a central component of the rehabilitation program.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic Foot Ulcer
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common antecedent to lower-limb amputation in high-income countries; contralateral ulcer prevention is a rehabilitation priority.

relatedConditions.name: Polyneuropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Present in the majority of dysvascular amputees; affects residual limb sensation, contralateral limb risk, and phantom pain patterns.

relatedConditions.name: Spinal Cord Injury Rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: A related Physical Medicine & Rehabilitation specialty; interdisciplinary team composition and rehabilitation milestones share methodology even as the clinical goals differ.

faqs.question: What is amputation rehabilitation?
faqs.answer: Amputation rehabilitation is the medical and therapeutic process of restoring function, mobility, and independence after limb removal. It spans from pre-operative education through wound healing, prosthetic fitting, gait training, and community reintegration. It is led by a physiatrist and includes prosthetists, physical therapists, occupational therapists, and psychologists.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after a below-knee amputation?
faqs.answer: Wound healing takes 4-8 weeks. Prosthetic fitting begins at 4-8 weeks when the residual limb is healed and volume-stable. Basic indoor prosthetic ambulation is typically achieved by 2-4 months. Community ambulation and return to work in sedentary roles is usually possible by 4-6 months, with continued improvement over the first year.

faqs.question: What percentage of amputees walk again?
faqs.answer: Approximately 60-70% of unilateral below-knee amputees achieve functional outdoor prosthetic ambulation at one year. Above-knee amputees achieve functional ambulation in 40-55%. Bilateral amputees and elderly dysvascular amputees have lower rates, often using wheelchairs alongside prosthetics. Traumatic amputees under 60 have the highest ambulation rates.

faqs.question: What is phantom limb pain?
faqs.answer: Phantom limb pain is pain perceived in a limb that has been amputated, affecting 60-80% of amputees. It ranges from mild cramping to severe burning or electric pain. First-line treatments are gabapentin or pregabalin and mirror visual feedback therapy. Most patients improve significantly with treatment; 30% resolve completely within one year.

faqs.question: How is phantom limb pain treated?
faqs.answer: Gabapentin or pregabalin (alpha-2-delta ligands) is the first-line medication. Mirror visual feedback therapy creates a visual illusion of the missing limb moving painlessly and reduces pain in 50-65% of patients. Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) is a useful adjunct. Amitriptyline and opioids are second- and third-line options.

faqs.question: What is a prosthetic leg and how long does it take to learn to use one?
faqs.answer: A prosthetic leg is a device replacing the amputated limb, attached via a fitted socket. Learning basic indoor walking takes 4-8 weeks of physical therapy for most below-knee amputees. Progressing to outdoor surfaces, stairs, and uneven terrain takes 3-6 months. Advanced prosthetic users achieve running and sports activities within 6-18 months.

faqs.question: What is a K-level in amputation?
faqs.answer: The K-level (K0-K4) is the Medicare Functional Classification System for prosthetic candidacy. K0 is non-ambulatory; K2 is household ambulation on flat surfaces; K3 is community ambulation on varied terrain; K4 is high-activity use including sports. K-level determines which prosthetic components (basic vs. microprocessor) insurance will fund.

faqs.question: What is osseointegration for amputees?
faqs.answer: Osseointegration is a surgical technique attaching a titanium implant directly into the residual bone; the prosthesis connects to the implant through the skin, eliminating the socket. It improves proprioception, eliminates skin breakdown from socket wear, and dramatically improves prosthetic tolerance. Implant survival is approximately 90% at 5 years.

faqs.question: Can you drive a car after a leg amputation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most lower-limb amputees can drive after rehabilitation, either with prosthesis or hand controls. A driving evaluation by an occupational therapist specializing in driving rehabilitation is required before returning to driving. Left-side amputees who drive automatic vehicles often return to driving with minimal modification.

faqs.question: What happens to the residual limb after amputation?
faqs.answer: The residual limb undergoes significant volume reduction and shaping in the first 3-6 months as post-operative edema resolves and muscles atrophy. Rigid removable dressings and compression shrinker socks manage edema and shape the limb for prosthetic fitting. Volume stabilizes over 6-12 months, after which a definitive prosthetic socket is made.

faqs.question: Does depression occur after amputation?
faqs.answer: Yes, depression and anxiety occur in 25-50% of new amputees, particularly in the first year. Risk factors include traumatic amputation cause, younger age, loss of occupation, and limited social support. Untreated depression significantly impairs rehabilitation engagement. Screening with PHQ-9 should occur at every rehabilitation visit.

faqs.question: What is targeted muscle reinnervation?
faqs.answer: Targeted muscle reinnervation (TMR) is a surgical technique that reroutes residual nerve branches to available muscle targets in the residual limb. It prevents painful neuroma formation and creates myoelectric signals that improve prosthetic control for upper-limb amputees. TMR reduces phantom pain in 80-90% of patients in case series.

faqs.question: How do I protect my remaining leg after an amputation?
faqs.answer: After lower-limb amputation, the contralateral limb bears increased mechanical load. Inspect it daily for blisters, wounds, or discoloration. See a podiatrist annually for nail care and footwear assessment. Manage diabetes, PAD, and blood pressure aggressively. 25-50% of dysvascular amputees face contralateral amputation within 3-5 years without preventive care.

faqs.question: What sports can amputees participate in?
faqs.answer: Amputees participate in a wide range of sports including swimming, cycling, running, wheelchair basketball, skiing, climbing, and golf. Activity-specific prostheses (running blades, swimming prostheses) are available for K4-level patients. Organizations like the Challenged Athletes Foundation provide equipment grants and adaptive sports programs.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate after amputation in diabetic patients?
faqs.answer: Dysvascular amputees with diabetes have a 5-year survival rate of only 40-60%, primarily driven by underlying cardiovascular disease rather than the amputation itself. Myocardial infarction and stroke are the leading causes of death. Aggressive management of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, and smoking is essential to improve survival.
